---
title: "R Notebook"
output: 
html_notebook
pdf_document: default
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code. 

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*. 

```{r}
data("sleep")
head(sleep)
```




```{r}
levels(sleep$group)
```
```{r}
#boxplot for visuals of the data
boxplot(
          formula=extra~group,
          data=sleep,
          horizontal=TRUE,
          col= c("red","blue"),
          xlab = ("Change in sleep(hours)"),
          ylab = "Group"
          )

```


From the boxplot, there are no outliers in each group. The distribution of sleep pattern for group 2 is more right-skewed than those for group 1. Group 1 recorded more negative values than group 2. We will go ahead with our tests to establish statistical significance.





```{r}
#test for normality
shapiro.test(
              sleep$extra
)
```

Since  the p-value is greater than alpha, we fail to reject the null hypothesis

CONCLUSION
The population from which the data is drawn is normally distributed.






```{r}
#test for equality of variance
var.test(sleep$extra[sleep$group == 1],
         sleep$extra[sleep$group == 2],
         alternative = "two.sided",
         conf.level = 0.95)
```


Since the p-value is greater than the significancelevel alpha, we fail to reject the null hypothesis of the variance test.

CONCLUSION
The variance of the population of both groups are equal.


```{r}
#hypothesis test
t.test(formula=extra~group,
       data=sleep,
       var.equal=TRUE)
```

Since the p-value of the t-test is greater than the significance level alpha, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.

CONCLUSION
The sample mean for group 2 is greater than the smaple mean of group 1. With 95% confidence, we say there is no statistically significant different in the average improvement of sleep patterns for both groups.
